rationale: Sustainable Retail Group qualifies for Article 8 on the strength of full Scope 1+2+3 emissions tracking, a 34% sustainable product range, and an active supplier audit programme. However, low board independence (2/8 directors), 16% supplier audit coverage (vs France Duty of Vigilance requirements), and an unresolved EU Deforestation Regulation exposure place this at the lower band of Article 8. The complex 8-country supply chain is the binding constraint for any Article 9 upgrade.

qualifyingFactors.assessment: Sustainable product range (34% of SKUs), full Scope 1+2+3 tracking, 61% recyclable packaging — clear promotional evidence
2/8 independent directors — below ESRS 40% threshold; board gender diversity 25% vs 40% target
Full Scope 1+2+3 data available — strong PAI foundation; biodiversity and pay equity missing

16% supplier audit coverage — France Duty of Vigilance (5,000 employees in France / 10,000 worldwide threshold) does NOT apply at 1,200 employees. However, EUDR due diligence is mandatory as first-placer of SE Asia commodities on EU market.
No formal sustainable investment definition or minimum percentage established at fund level

SE Asia supply chain creates high commodity deforestation risk — compliance gap identified. EUDR enforcement for large operators: 30 December 2026 (second delay enacted Dec 2025). ~9 months to achieve compliance.

principalAdverseImpacts.gap: Scope 3 dominates — Category 1 supplier emissions require full audit trail beyond current 16% coverage
Above retail sector median (290) — Science-Based Target required for Article 9 credibility
Below retail sector median of 38% — renewable energy transition plan required
SE Asia sourcing (palm oil, timber, rubber) creates high deforestation risk — EU Deforestation Regulation effective December 2025
Below ESRS 40% gender parity target — board recruitment required before Article 8 is defensible
France Duty of Vigilance requires documented process for all Tier 1 suppliers — current coverage is insufficient
Multi-geography operations elevate FCPA/UK Bribery Act exposure — policy required, PAI indicator 15

requiredActions: Expand board independence to minimum 4/8 directors — appoint sustainability-qualified NED immediately
Scale supplier audit programme from 16% to ≥60% (France Duty of Vigilance compliance)
Complete EU Deforestation Regulation commodity mapping for SE Asia supply chains before December 2025 deadline
Establish Science-Based Targets (SBTi) for Scope 3 reduction — required for Article 9 credibility
Define sustainable investment minimum (recommend ≥34% — aligned to current sustainable product mix)
Implement DNSH screening for EU Taxonomy retail activities (NACE G47)
upgradePath.estimatedTimeToUpgrade: 24–36 months — supply chain complexity and Deforestation Regulation compliance are binding constraints

disclosureRequirements.action: Publish fund-level disclosure before first LP close — SFDR Level 2 RTS Article 16

disclosureRequirements.requirement: France Duty of Vigilance (Loi de Vigilance)
disclosureRequirements.action: Threshold is 5,000 employees in France OR 10,000 worldwide — Sustainable Retail (1,200 employees) is below threshold and NOT legally required to publish a vigilance plan. Voluntary supply chain due diligence is still best practice for LP confidence.

Map commodity exposure (palm oil, cocoa, timber, rubber) in SE Asia supply chain — large-operator deadline 30 December 2026 (second delay enacted Dec 2025). Only first-placer on EU market responsible under simplified rules.
GHG and labour data available; biodiversity, water, and anti-corruption gaps require immediate data collection

lpNarrative: Sustainable Retail Group supports an SFDR Article 8 classification on the strength of its full Scope 1+2+3 emissions data, 34% sustainable product range, and active supplier audit programme. However, the low board independence (25%) and EU Deforestation Regulation exposure in the SE Asia supply chain require immediate remediation before this classification is fully defensible to scrutinising LPs. For LPs with strict Article 8 criteria — including those following PRI's responsible investment framework — we recommend a 100-day governance and supply chain remediation sprint before LP close. The Article 9 upgrade pathway exists but requires 24–36 months given supply chain complexity.
regulatoryRisk: Medium — EUDR (large-operator deadline 30 December 2026) is the primary active risk — SE Asia commodity sourcing requires geographic coordinates and due diligence statements within ~9 months. CSRD: post-Omnibus I (March 2026), Sustainable Retail (1,200 employees, €150M revenue) does NOT meet the €450M+ revenue threshold — OUT of mandatory CSRD scope. France Duty of Vigilance: threshold is 5,000 employees in France OR 10,000 worldwide — Sustainable Retail NOT in scope. CSDDD: 5,000+ employees + €1.5B threshold — NOT in scope. SFDR 2.0: 'Sustainable Retail Group' brand name at risk post-2028 if Non-categorised — EUDR compliance is critical to maintaining UNGC/OECD compliance status. Despite being out of most mandatory EU reporting scopes, LP pressure for voluntary ESG disclosure remains — Nordic LPs will require SFDR 2.0 category compliance for fund access.

sfdr2.rationale: Sustainable Retail Group faces the most challenging SFDR 2.0 transition in the portfolio. Despite its Article 8 label under 1.0, the reality is stark: 76% of its GHG emissions sit in unmanaged Scope 3, board independence falls below the mandatory 40% threshold, and the EU Deforestation Regulation creates an active compliance breach. Under SFDR 2.0, the 'promotes characteristics' framing is gone — this fund would land at ESG Basics at best. The ≥70% Transition threshold cannot be met when the majority of supply chain emissions are untracked and unmanaged. The UNGC/OECD compliance status is at risk given the EUDR exposure and labour standards gaps in SE Asia operations. Without urgent remediation, Non-categorised is a realistic worst-case outcome that would disqualify the fund from virtually all European LP mandates.

threshold70pct.currentEstimate: ~20–25% — sustainable product SKUs (34%) provide some basis, but 76% unmanaged Scope 3 and absent supply chain audit coverage prevent any credible Transition alignment claim
threshold70pct.gap: Scope 3 Category 1 full audit coverage (currently 16%), SBTi commitment with credible supply chain decarbonisation roadmap, EUDR commodity compliance — all required before threshold is claimable

taxonomyShortcut.taxonomyAlignment: Estimated <5% — retail CapEx not documented against EU Taxonomy climate mitigation criteria (NACE G47). Sustainable packaging investments may contribute marginally, but DNSH screening is absent.

keyChanges: Article 8 label is eliminated — Sustainable Retail migrates to ESG Basics, a weaker classification signal
UNGC/OECD compliance is now mandatory for ALL categories — SE Asia labour practices create baseline risk
The 70% threshold is unachievable without full Scope 3 audit and supply chain decarbonisation — Transition category is 36+ months away
Non-categorised is a realistic risk if EUDR breach triggers OECD Guidelines violation finding
gaps: EUDR compliance (SE Asia palm oil, timber, rubber) — mandatory exclusion risk if UNGC/OECD status fails
Full Scope 3 supplier audit coverage — current 16% is incompatible with any threshold claim
Board independence to ≥40% — mandatory governance baseline under SFDR 2.0
SBTi commitment with 1.5°C-aligned supply chain plan — required for Transition category aspiration
Anti-corruption policy covering all 8 operating geographies
Biodiversity / EUDR commodity mapping — deforestation exposure is both a mandatory exclusion risk and a PAI gap
sfdr2.migrationTimeline: URGENT: Begin EUDR commodity mapping immediately to avoid Non-categorised risk (UNGC/OECD compliance at risk). Target ESG Basics by 2028 as the floor. Transition category requires 3-4 years of supply chain transformation — start Scope 3 audit programme and SBTi process in 2026 to have any realistic path.

marketingRisk.nameRisk: CRITICAL: The company is named 'Sustainable Retail Group' — the word 'Sustainable' is directly in the brand name. Under SFDR 2.0, if this fund is classified as Non-categorised (a realistic outcome given EUDR breach risk and UNGC/OECD at-risk status), it would be LEGALLY PROHIBITED from using 'Sustainable' in the fund name or any marketing materials. This means a forced rebranding of the entire entity — a severe commercial and reputational consequence.
marketingRisk.implication: The brand name 'Sustainable Retail Group' becomes a liability under SFDR 2.0 if Non-categorised. Estimated rebranding costs: €500K-€2M (legal, marketing, signage, digital assets across 8 countries). More critically, any LP that subscribed based on the 'Sustainable' brand name would have grounds for complaint. Immediate action: achieve ESG Basics compliance by 2028 to preserve naming rights at minimum level.

paiObligations.flexibility: As an ESG Basics fund, no product-level PAI reporting required under SFDR 2.0. However, Sustainable Retail's existing Scope 1+2+3 data and supplier audit programme are assets that should be voluntarily disclosed — the framework permits voluntary sustainability factor disclosures even for non-categorised products. Use this data proactively to support the ESG Basics classification and build toward Transition.
paiObligations.complianceCostImpact: Estimated 30-40% reduction in SFDR compliance costs at ESG Basics level — no entity-level PAI, no product-level PAI, simplified 2-page disclosure only. However, EUDR and France Duty of Vigilance compliance costs remain and are increasing. Net compliance burden is NOT decreasing — regulatory pressure is merely shifting from SFDR to supply chain regulation.
